open_supported_exploration:
Being able to quickly see how changing subclasses affects a high level character (rather than checking level by level like in the game, or seeing the info seperately on a wiki) encouraged me to experiment and led me to change most of the characters quite a bit.

The tool made it helpful to see the different damage types and made me not focus too much on a single type (e.g. making a necro warlock makes you want to pick those spells, but after seeing that the character had little variety made me diversify the spells)

The main usefulness was in seeing the spread of damage types, ranges and utility vs damage.

Being able to see changes quickly (e.g. changing races, subraces, subclasses) and how it affects the character made me look at more choices than I usually would sinc eit so quick.
open_blocked_exploration: A minor downside of planning a fully leveled character is failing to see exactly which choices one will make at each level so as to have a clear progression during gameplay, but I feel that is easy to figure out during gameplay as you can see the choices available upon level up.
open_changed_normal_planning: Since I usually do not use third party tools but wikis instead, this made the information much easier to access (everything is in one place), and you can see how the different choices affect each other.
open_suggested_change:
Improve readability

Some of the text in the wheel is too small so a bit harder to read than I would like. 

About understanding the visualization, I did fully understand (I think) the visualization, but only after playing with it for some time and not immediately.
open_optional_comments:
Exploration/history comment: Regarding the tool helping me check previous version, I put 'neither' as I did not use the feature. When making single characters it would probably be less useful since the complexity of any one character is not very high. It would probably be more useful when making in depth experimentation of a full party, which I did not do here.

Autonomy and optimization comment: Regarding the task not feeling like optimisation: I agree with the statement and the tool does not inherently push towards maximising damage (no rankings, showing which options do more damage etc.), though having direct feedback when making a choice (seeing the number go up and also the circle getting bigger) makes it more likely to keep damaging spells instead of support spells. Still those are useful features that are worth having despite this.

Feature usefulness comment: I did not find myself using the small party member circles to check on the other members much, but I think that is because I just made them now rather than having characters I made a while ago, as I remembered all the choices I made with them. 

For the build history, as I focused more on the individual characters rather than making many party iterations I also did not use this feature much,

Did not use the simulator.

Missing information comment: None that I could think of (as in I never yearned for information and could not find it).
open_factor_visualization_observation: Seeing the spread of different damage ranges and damage types made me diversify the characters more (or focus them in case of ranged only characters where I did not care for non ranged options, though in those cases it reminded me to select utility spells for escaping back into desired range)

open_supported_exploration: Yes I tried to average and spread the damage information over the 4 team characters.
open_blocked_exploration: The dropdown lists when selecting characteristics. I think that the lack of explanations for each characteristic was missing for the reason why to choose it.
open_changed_normal_planning: That you have a list of saved characters that you can save and load on the sidebar.
open_factor_visualization_observation: Yes I tried to average and spread the damage information over the 4 team characters.

open_supported_exploration: The part that was most helpful for me was the visualization and the ability to interact with it. It allowed me to easily follow the results, identify where my party was unbalanced, and fix it right away.
open_blocked_exploration:
I think the drop-down menus slightly blocked the exploration process, because someone new to the game might not be familiar with the classes, backgrounds, subclasses, and other options.

They would need to search online to better understand the options and choose their characters’ traits wisely.

I believe the ability improvement feature was a very good example of how a short explanation can help clarify the outcome of these choices.
open_changed_normal_planning:
I just follow guides if I'm trying to Min-Max a build. 

However, If I want to experiment and make my own build, I would use this tool to do so and to see where the build is rough around the edges.
open_suggested_change: As I mentioned before, adding a short explanation to each drop-down menu option would be helpful for new users to follow. It would also serve as a useful reminder for experienced users, helping them understand how the differences between options could affect the final outcome and choose the traits that best suit their vision for the characters.
open_optional_comments:
I really liked the accessibility of the tool and the ability to easily see and change all of my trait choices in one place.

I also liked that the tool matched the theme of the game, which made it feel more connected to the overall experience. It really looked like it was part of the game, rather than something detached from it.

open_supported_exploration: The visualization is very intuitive and helps in exploring strengths and weaknesses of the party
open_blocked_exploration: First time opening the tool is a little overwhelming and takes a bit of getting used to
open_changed_normal_planning: Personally I prefer to build my party based on roleplay, but this helps me visualize the build I created
open_suggested_change: It would be nice to see even more information about the build, for example some algorithm that recommends what to change

open_supported_exploration: The initial tutorial
open_blocked_exploration: overlapping text or cut out
open_changed_normal_planning: It was my first time
open_suggested_change: expandable side menus

open_supported_exploration: I went into this with zero knowledge on Baldur's Gate 3, so I did not know anything. I used the circle on the right to understand the abilities of each class/race (e.g., whether they did fire damage or acid damage or how the extent of their utility). I noticed on my second character that there were differences per race, so I tried to make my third and fourth character based on race. This was to maybe fill in the gaps that could be there. So, I basically looked more at the third tab when setting up a character at this point.
open_blocked_exploration: Whenever I wanted to try out my build it said that the server was occupied. So, I did not get to actually try out many things or see the synergy between my characters. I just used my usual playstyle in games to assess what I would like my party to have.
open_changed_normal_planning: I think I was more aware of everything. I usually avoid details when I plan a party or build a character. I prefer to just play the party to see what I think about it and if the party achieves what I made it for. I have the same with spells where I just want to try them out or watch a video to see what they do. When I build characters I always look up a guide if it is not provided in the game. So, I usually don't really think about it in this much detail. I liked it though. I thought it was pleasant to do and was curious how good the party that I was creating would be in the game.
open_suggested_change: I would add more explanations in the form of tooltips especially for the ability scores. I had to search up a lot of definitions and what would be good for my character. So, I would have liked it if the tool explained to me what Wisdom, Intelligence, Charisma, History, Athletics, Perception, etc. would change to my character.
open_optional_comments: My interest in Baldur's Gate 3 increased while making my characters mainly because I was curious about what these characters would be like in game.

open_supported_exploration: Ease of use
open_blocked_exploration: There's a bit of a learning curve
open_changed_normal_planning: not much, I used it for fun and I usually don't optimise builds
open_suggested_change: Maybe warnings on significant overlap?

open_supported_exploration: Being able to save builds and return to them was super helpful for comparison and iteration since I did not have to worry about losing my previous choices when experimenting.
open_blocked_exploration:
The circle visualization is not clear as to how it is meant to be used. An example of its design purpose and thought process would go a long way.
Moreover, it would be nice to be able to see how the party will evolve in the long term. This evolution happens over a significant period of real time, so it would be useful to visualize what this would look like when designing a party.
open_changed_normal_planning: I do not generally plan that far ahead. This tool made it quite engaging and motivating to try this process out.
open_suggested_change: It would be nice to be able to see how the party will evolve in the long term. This evolution happens over a significant period of real time, so it would be useful to visualize what this would look like when designing a party.
open_optional_comments: Awesome study! Looking forward to the results.

open_supported_exploration: The ability to oversee all abilities in one diagram, as well as of the whole party
open_blocked_exploration: A way to easily see what all different subclasses give as in abilities.
open_changed_normal_planning: I could make choises planning ahead in levels and not just make decisions as I level up.
open_suggested_change: It was hard to switch between party members as I also accidentally deleted 1 of my saved builds. Make that more easy. And add a confirmation question if you delete or overwrite a saved build.

open_supported_exploration: Having all the icons and explanations from BG3 feels familiar and its easy to immediately choose from memory. Similarity to D&D Beyond also helps.
open_blocked_exploration: Cluttered and tiny sections of the screen
open_changed_normal_planning: Did not differ at all
open_suggested_change: Add: Less cluttering and more easily accessible. Ability to click and drag across the cycle in a more zoomed in way.

open_supported_exploration: As someone who has never played BG3 or a DND campaign, the tooltips for skills, abilities and other unknowns were a great help.
open_blocked_exploration: Tooltips again. They were a good resource to fill my lack of understanding, but I also felt that some things weren't explained (such as the class choice on ranger like preferred enemy).
open_changed_normal_planning: I can only offer perspective as someone who used planners for other games. As such I used my usual approach to build planning when using this tool.
open_suggested_change: I don't feel knowledgeable enough about BG to comment.
open_optional_comments: I couldn't use the optional feature to test my build :( hopefully it works later.

open_supported_exploration: knowing all the options from which to choose from
open_blocked_exploration: when choosing the spells, it was a bit overwhelming going through all because the text was in the way and changed rapidly
open_changed_normal_planning: you can change things quicker and more easily, having a better overview of characters
open_suggested_change: have a separate fixed box to describe all the spells

open_supported_exploration: the focus rings
open_blocked_exploration: none
open_changed_normal_planning: not
open_suggested_change: not

open_supported_exploration: Explaining each choice and ability in a pop up helps returning players remember quickly what each option is and allows new players to flip though all available options.
open_blocked_exploration: The visualization was a bit overwhelming but not overall discouraging.
open_changed_normal_planning: It helped keep everything contained. Having every option, ability etc available at a moments notice is very helpful and being able to switch between them is great, Personally, I mostly go with trial and error. I start with a more roleplay focused team and fix my team's weaknesses as I play the game, respecing what need respecing and changing subclasses to fit my needs.
open_suggested_change: I think it's a very neat tool. I don't have much to add, maybe just a better, in depth explanation of the visualization system but otherwise nothing. I like it.

open_supported_exploration: Having a bird's eye view of all the possible spells I could pick helped greatly. Being able to quickly refer back to always prepared spells was great to ensure there was no overlap in my choices
open_blocked_exploration: The lack of gear options and multiclassing. I was hoping to make a piercing damage centered hexblade-rogue using Bhaalist armor and crimson mischief and knife of the undermountain king, but the tool does not provide this option yet
open_changed_normal_planning: The characters were much more open-ended. There aren't any short race origin characters in BG3. (Not that I'd use them, the lower movement speed feels terrible)
open_suggested_change: I'd add multiclassing and gear options, along with a fourth character slot. It was a bit confusing how I was supposed to submit the full party
open_optional_comments: I'm more into action rpg's. I felt a bit out of my depth using a build planner for a more tactical rpg like Baldur's gate 3

open_supported_exploration: The most influential tool has to be the wheel, as it helped me see numerous mistakes or areas for improvement. It is a creative way to show the key elements of your team.
open_blocked_exploration: As much as I like and praise the wheel, I also feel like it is hard to manage with it at first glance. It's way of displaying is daunting and confusing at first. It's initial explanation is fine, but I feel as though it could've been explained more thoroughly.
open_changed_normal_planning: I would usually leave out or forget to account for team synergy and key aspects of the characters. This tool helped me take all of that into account, making my builds synergize much more.
open_suggested_change: The visualization is both pleasing and fitting of the genre, but for the sake of easier navigation I would tone down some of the layout and simplify the wheel.

open_supported_exploration: The visual aspect for sure.
open_blocked_exploration: I just wished there was more written feedback and sometimes the way of navigating between members can be slightly confusing.
open_changed_normal_planning: Low key I go based off of vibes so it was definitely helpful.
open_suggested_change: I feel like the tool is good for visualizing but I wish there was more feedback, like written feedback about party interactions and stuff

open_supported_exploration: Spell selection was clearly outlined over each level
open_blocked_exploration: Lack of multi-class options.
open_changed_normal_planning: Better visualisation of class abilities.
open_suggested_change: Ability to add companion specific abilities to a build (eg, Astarion bite).

open_supported_exploration: The visual of the circle chart was really helpful. I was able to clearly understand what kind of spells the party had most of, which I think will help when it comes to strategizing in-game.
open_blocked_exploration: Nothing felt like it was blocking my creativity.
open_changed_normal_planning: I normally build as I play and very rarely plan ahead. This was very different for me, but a lot of fun!
open_suggested_change: I would like to know if any spells overlap between the characters. It would also be helpful to have spells that are always prepared greyed out on the spell selector.
open_factor_other: I based each character off of the PCs in the real-life DnD campaign I am currently part of.

open_supported_exploration: The building tool is great for showing what your character has access to at different levels, and saving builds you like for a later playthrough
open_blocked_exploration: Using this tool it made it harder for me to imagine my character as a real person in a fantasy world, as opposed to a bunch of numbers on a sheet.
open_changed_normal_planning: I did not change it much, I already build my characters in a similar way, only using different resources like wikis
open_suggested_change: The data visualization did not help me in the slightest. I feel like the way you intend to use certain abilities and how often has such a great effect on their role, that a general visualization can never do it justice. At later levels it also became far to cluttered to have any real use.
open_optional_comments: I used the character builder to replicate my current builds, rather than create new ones, so the results might be affected by that.

open_supported_exploration: The aggregation circle is the most helpful one for me. It was really nice to see all the abilities in one place and be able to hover over them and compare them.
open_blocked_exploration: One thing that threw me off is the damage calculation. I am not sure it's done correctly. For example, for the Rogue, when I hover over the sneak attack, both melee and ranged show an average of 3.5 (which correlates to a dice roll between 1-6). But the sneak attack dice should be a 6d6, which I would also expect to give a higher than average damage output because I chose the savage attacker feat in the end (as the description of the feat is: "When making melee weapon attacks, you roll your damage dice twice and use the highest result").  I haven't checked the other damage calculations, but comparison becomes difficult if they are not calculated correctly.
open_changed_normal_planning: I don't think it made things that much more different for me. I generally enjoy having martial or melee classes. I can make an alright sorcerer or warlock build if I want to, but they just don't hit the same ;)
open_suggested_change:
Well, the only thing that threw me off was the damage calculation. I really enjoy the way that the abilities are displayed and the proximity circles are really cleverly made in my opinion (so the self / melee / range). It kind of makes me wonder if I can make a party that only has actions/attacks in the melee range, but I'm a bit short on time. 
I think it would be cool to also be able to see somewhere the average initiative rolls for the party. I think high initiative is quite important in BG3 so I usually try to have at least one or two party members with the alert feat.
open_optional_comments: It's a bit late to implement, but every time I wanted to double check something about my build or the tool I had to close the survey and my answers wouldn't be saved. It made it a bit difficult to give extensive feedback, but I hope it's still helpful.

open_supported_exploration: I really do like the color coding with the circle on the right to get a pretty good idea of what any character is capable of, which I could immediately compare to the rest of the party.
open_blocked_exploration: I myself was a bit overwhelmed with the explanation on how to use this tool. Because of that, this tool felt far more complicated than it actually is, at least at the start.
open_changed_normal_planning: As opposed to bg3, it helped me get a more clear picture of what my characters could do far more quickly then switching between characters in bg3
open_suggested_change: For me, the 16-step tutorial felt a bit overwhelming. I'd probably try to make it a toggle-able function where after pressing it, you could hover with your cursor over the distinct parts of this tool to then get information on how to use it.
open_optional_comments: With that all being said, this tool is amazing. I don't think I have ever seen something this polished and visually distinct for bg3 party builders.

open_supported_exploration: Its easy visualization of strenghts, skills, and damage elements in graphic form. This helps the player test team synergy, and lets them see to see what skills belong to what class and experiment, creating different teams. The freedom to give a name to your character and build made it feel more personal and fun. Players will more easily remember the parties they want to build in game with this tool.
open_blocked_exploration: I don't have experience with dnd or Baldur's gate 3 so im not familiar with luck based combat, manipulating with creatures or anything like that so I couldn't understand how what im doing exactly affects my gameplay but that is not the tool's fault. :)
open_changed_normal_planning: I usually look up character builds on YouTube or ask my friends on how to build characters. If the tool was designed for the games I play i would use it all the time to visualize party synergy, see what i am missing in my team, check what certain enemies do to different characters and vice versa, and the damage I would be doing.
open_suggested_change: No, I think everything is great! :)

open_supported_exploration: The ease of use and how quickly you can change the character made the exploration enjoyable and thus encouraged me to try different combinations (since it provides quick and direct feedback on the impacts through the wheel).
open_blocked_exploration: Nothing specific to the tool inhibited exploration. Existing knowledge of the game made me avoid certain skills, spells and similar if I knew them to be bad or ineffective to such a degree that picking them made the build weaker without contributing much in terms of fun, diversity or roleplay opportunities ( e.g. no amount of exploration and tinkering will make true strike worth picking).
open_changed_normal_planning: Usually I would make the characters directly within the game using knowledge from the wiki or my own knowledge, and keep remaking them until I am satisfied. That is much slower (done level by level instead of directly all at once) and provides no direct feedback on the effects of my choices until they are put into practice actually playing the game.
open_suggested_change: There are no superfluous features (and none that make the process more difficult), so nothing should be removed. In this case I did not find myself using the build history feature as much, but I can imagine it being useful in some cases and it is non intrusive so I would keep it.

open_supported_exploration: I really loved the connection between selecting things such as the damage type and then being able to see in a more detailed way the spells that had it. For example i was trying to copy a character from another game, and i felt it was easier to do so because i could more easily see which spells were psychic necrotic etc.
open_blocked_exploration: I wish items in the build planner had more detailed descriptions. also maybe a list of all damage types or something, because i had to first find the first of the damage type, and only then i could select it to highlight.
open_changed_normal_planning: I really like roleplaying, and i felt it was way easier now to select and filter stuff that i found necessary to the character i was trying to build.
open_suggested_change: Id really like if terms that are more abstract were added to descriptions. like i was trying to build a gothic lovecraftian mad scholar, i wish arms of hadar had  a tag "eldritch" or something so that i could quickly find stuff that was relevant to that type of character flavor.

open_supported_exploration: i found the data circle really insightful, as i eel like you lose the sense of coverage in normal game. like i can hover over an ability and se ah these 2 are psychic, but it wont register in my brain like it did here that  "oh snap, there are actually so little psychic abilities for the wizard, i have to try some other build". for example, I also really dont often think about like aoe spread. in actual gameplay, i feel like i make my life harder because i pick aoe abilities because i just think they are cool, and i dont look at if they're aoe or single target and then i regret my life because i make a chunk of the floor be damaging to me as well as the enemy, but  then i also have msotly meelee charachters that now just cannot engage in meanigful combat. this tool allowed me to more easily then see like, ah, in the aggregate view, i have a lot meelee guys, but also a lot of area damage, lets change that so i have long range, or less area damage.
open_blocked_exploration: Equipment is a big part of it, i wish i had equipment that i could evaluate, because i find myself often making builds in accordance to the outfit and its abilities.  Like i googled it myself, some things that i knew relate dto what i wanted to. but i feel that i would explore even more if i had a list of possible equpment choices for certain builds. i also wish that icons in agregate view indicated where they come from.
open_changed_normal_planning: I usually only look at guides, this felt like i had my plans in my own hands, and i didn't feel pushed to google the highest damage magic missile build. i felt as if ti shifted my perspective from lets do highest magic missile build to lets see how force builds perform.
open_suggested_change: It might bee too cluttered but i feel like id want the visualisation to also show me what am i missing, or some other part, like what damage types am i not covering or what utility roles am i not covering. it works for range because all the possibilies you can see are always clearly there but for damage types and roles i do not know what should be there. yknow.

open_supported_exploration: The most useful part for me as I was mostlz focusing on combat optimisation is easily sorting throuh damage types and ranges (the highlight feature) and easily seeing which spells are concentration spells so as to not put too many on one character. Also seeing the spread of damage types across the whole party helps diversify the damage types (or do the opposite and focus on one if I know im preparing for a specific zone or enocunter).
open_blocked_exploration: No part of the tool felt like it was 'blocking' exploration. Because of how quick you can change a character I did not use the history feature much (rather than digging through the history I just go and change back whatever I need). I could see it being useful in a very long session or if I pause for a long time and have to remind myself what I was doing.
open_changed_normal_planning: I dont usually use third party software as Im familiar enough with the game to not feel the need for one. This was nice as e.g. if I have some ideas for characters that I want to build and have somewhere without needing to use them in the game immediately (as in game you cannot have 'reserve' builds, you build a character and have to play with it), this is a nice 'idea storage'.
open_suggested_change: Adding multiclassing would be nice as it is a feature in-game.

open_supported_exploration: I really loved that it was all connected in a circle, usually I use data visualizations for WOW and i find it difficult sometimes to take into account, mentally (comprehend them as a union of things rather than separate charts) separate charts. I felt like its circluar structure made it really easy to understand and compare, since it was easy to get used to and understand when you got the feel for it.
open_blocked_exploration: I'm new to the game, i wish there were more detailed descriptions in the build planner. i liked that the circle was mostly overview but i really wanted richer information in the build planner. I found myself going to wikis a lot, which was  a good thing i guess because i had fun and learned a lot about the game, so i guess id like there to my wiki hyperlinks next to some elements.
open_changed_normal_planning: i felt it was easier to compare between build more than I am used to (I mostly use tools for WoW). its hard to get used to barcharts for this then in the there window is this, now compare each build. this way i feel as its connected, and takes the least amount of effort from me once i get a hold of how it works. i also like that the layers were similar with the percentiles.
open_suggested_change: maybe custom notes in the process view, so i can customize my experience even further as i play the game. i could take note of equipment quests etc and like write who did good where or elements of the build that weren't good that i should change. like to give an explanation of why this build in the process wasnt working, for my self.
